review_summary: This unit supports mastery questions on scaled dot-product attention, matrix-vectorized attention, and the separation of keys and values. No visual-only details are needed to assess the core ideas.

question_bank.question: Why does scaled dot-product attention divide dot products by the square root of the vector dimension?
choices: To keep softmax inputs from becoming too large as dimension grows, preserving useful gradients.
To force every query to attend uniformly to all keys.
To remove the need for learned key and value projections.
To make the output sequence shorter than the input sequence.
question_bank.explanation: Dot products tend to grow in magnitude with dimension. Scaling keeps the values entering softmax in a healthier range, which helps avoid saturated probabilities and weak gradients.

question_bank.question: In the key-value view of attention, what is the difference between keys and values?
choices: Keys are used to match queries; values are the vectors combined to form the output.
Keys are the final outputs; values are discarded after softmax.
Keys store token positions; values store only punctuation marks.
Keys are fixed constants; values are the only learned parameters.
question_bank.explanation: Attention separates lookup from retrieval. Queries compare against keys to decide attention weights, and those weights are then applied to values to construct the output representation.

question_bank.question: What makes cross-attention different from self-attention?
choices: The queries and the data being attended to can come from different sources.
It never uses softmax to compute attention weights.
It requires all input vectors to be identical before projection.
It only works when the input sequence has length one.
question_bank.explanation: Cross-attention is useful when one representation needs to look up information from another representation, such as output tokens attending to source tokens or text attending to image features.
